channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Seven Takeaways From the Clintons’ Epstein Depositions
item.description: Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton was defiant. Former President Bill Clinton spoke of President Trump’s ties to Epstein. A Republican raised a conspiracy theory.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Hillary Clinton speaking to reporters after her closed-door deposition before the House Oversight Committee, as part of its investigation into Jeffrey Epstein, in Chappaqua, N.Y.

item.title: Melania Trump Promotes ‘Peace Through Education’ at U.N. as War With Iran Expands
item.description: The first lady advocated an increased focus on education and empathy, as her husband has waged war in the Middle East and sought to cut education funding at home.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon/Reuters
item.media:description: Melania Trump struck the gavel as she presided over a United Nations Security Council meeting, at the U.N. headquarters in New York City on Monday.

item.title: Texas Senate Candidates Make Final Arguments Before Primary Elections
item.description: The Republicans John Cornyn and Ken Paxton — and the Democrats Jasmine Crockett and James Talarico — are battling in bitter and expensive races.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn greeted by supporters at a campaign event in Schertz, Texas, on Monday.

item.title: Can These Democrats Make Combative Centrism Happen for 2028 Election?
item.description: Third Way, the moderate Democratic advocacy group and think tank, has an expansive and expensive plan to shape the 2028 presidential campaign.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Meg Kinnard/Associated Press
item.media:description: Jon Cowan, left, president of the group Third Way, and Jim Messina, a Democratic strategist.

item.title: Trump Says He Will End Boycott of White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The mentalist Oz Pearlman is this year’s speaker. “Hopefully, it will be something very Special,” the president wrote on Truth Social.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Marine Corps Band playing at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner in Washington in 2024.

item.title: The messy primary fight for North Carolina’s brightest blue bubble.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina

item.title: Trump Embraces U.S. Military Power After Years of Caution
item.description: The wars in Afghanistan and Iraq offered a stark lesson in the limits of military force. The Iran attacks suggest an era of postwar wariness is over.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump in Corpus Christi, Texas, last week. His sudden embrace of U.S. military power is a stunning reversal.

item.title: Trump Gambles His Presidency in His War With Iran
item.description: The risks for President Trump from the assault on Iran are escalating as casualties mount, oil prices rise and the war expands across the region.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump leaving the White House on Friday. Interventions in the Middle East have bedeviled generations of American presidents.

item.title: Why Eliminating Iran’s Ballistic Missiles Could Be Difficult
item.description: Much of Tehran’s arsenal of missiles may be hidden underground, making it harder to destroy.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Trump Administration Abandons Efforts to Impose Executive Orders on Law Firms
item.description: The move amounts to a surrender in a clash that has led many law firms to submit to the president rather than face the threat of his executive orders.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department told the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia on Monday that it was no longer interested in pursuing the cases contesting executive orders barring law firms from government business.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Religious Parents, Blocking California’s Trans Student Policy
item.description: Christian teachers and parents challenged the state’s policies, which they say require schools to hide students’ transgender status from their parents.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: A protester holding a transgender flag outside the Supreme Court last month.

item.title: In First Appearance Since Taking the Country to War, Trump Is Business as Usual
item.description: President Trump gave a “brief update” on the Iran operation. And then he stayed true to form.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump at the White House on Monday, his first appearance in public since the U.S. attacked Iran this weekend.

item.title: See when your state holds its primary.
item.dc:creator: Alex Lemonides and Lily Boyce
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: In Republican Win, Supreme Court Retains G.O.P. District in New York
item.description: In an emergency ruling, the justices preserved the district of a Republican congresswoman, despite a lower-court ruling that it illegally diluted the power of minority voters.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Adam Gray for The New York Times
item.media:description: A voter at a polling center on Staten Island in October.

item.title: Can Trump Legally Kill Iran’s Leader?
item.description: The United States and Israel started a war with Iran by killing its supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A woman holding a photo of Ayatollah Khamenei during a rally in Tehran last month.

item.title: Track the battle to reshape the congressional maps.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: They’re Hiring at U.S.A.I.D. Just Not Anyone Who Worked There.
item.description: The Trump administration said in a memo it wanted to “avoid the risk of impaired objectivity” by hiring former staff members to wind down operations at the U.S. Agency for International Development.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former staff members and supporters of the U.S. Agency for International Development held a rally last month in Washington to mark one year since the agency was largely dismantled.

item.title: What Is Trump’s Neck Rash? His Doctor Declines to Explain.
item.description: President Trump’s physician did not explain what the skin condition was or what medication he was using to treat it.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Monday.

item.title: How Religion Is Playing in the Senate Democratic Primary in Texas
item.description: James Talarico is talking up his beliefs and his status as a seminary student. Jasmine Crockett, his opponent, is the daughter of a pastor and is steeped in the Black church.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative James Talarico greeting supporters last week during a campaign event in College Station, Texas.

item.title: Aaron Spencer Is Charged With Murder. He’s Also Running for County Sheriff in Arkansas.
item.description: Aaron Spencer has been charged with fatally shooting a man accused of assaulting Mr. Spencer’s daughter. But ahead of his trial, he’s running for county sheriff in Arkansas.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:description: A screengrab from a campaign video by Aaron Spencer.

item.title: As Maduro and Khamenei Learned, It’s Harder Than Ever for Leaders to Hide
item.description: A surge in sensors and cameras, combined with artificial intelligence, has transformed U.S. intelligence’s ability to locate foreign heads of state. Add to that an American president willing to capture or kill them.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro was captured by U.S. forces in January after American intelligence was able to locate him at an apartment inside a Caracas military base.

item.title: North Carolina House Primary Includes Divisions on Iran War, Israel and A.I.
item.description: A new ad from a progressive challenger, Nida Allam, attacks Representative Valerie Foushee over financial backing she has received from defense contractors and the A.I. industry.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Valerie Foushee of North Carolina, a Democrat, is facing a progressive challenger.

item.title: Investigators Examine Iran War as Possible Motive in Austin Shooting
item.description: The shooting happened about 26 hours after the United States and Israel attacked Iran. Officials identified the three who died in the shooting.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Fuller
item.media:credit: Ricardo B. Brazziell/Austin American-Statesman, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers near the scene of the shooting on Sunday in Austin.

item.title: Prosecutor Says Father ‘Could Have Prevented’ Georgia School Shooting
item.description: Before closing arguments in the trial, the father, Colin Gray, testified that he never considered his son capable of the attack that killed four people in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas and Alessandro Marazzi Sassoon
item.media:credit: Hyosub Shin/Atlanta Journal-Constitution, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Colin Gray, the father of the suspect in the Apalachee High School shooting, listens during his trial in Winder, Ga.

item.title: Here Are the Texas Races Where Latino Voters May Play a Critical Role
item.description: As Hispanic voters in the state have moved toward Republicans, both parties are trying to figure out the best way to appeal to them.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A voting sign in both English and Spanish seen at a polling site in McAllen, Texas, last month.

item.title: Texas Holds First Elections Since Starting the Gerrymandering War
item.description: The state’s congressional map looks much different, and so do those in other states across the country.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Early voting last week in Bryan, Texas. Republicans redrew the state’s House map last year at the behest of President Trump in an effort to give the party more seats.

item.title: How Trump Decided to Go to War With Iran
item.description: President Trump’s embrace of military action in Iran was spurred by an Israeli leader determined to end diplomatic negotiations. Few of the president’s advisers voiced opposition.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti, Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager, Edward Wong, Eric Schmitt and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times

item.title: Texas Senate Primary Becomes Most Expensive in History
item.description: The primary race for Senate in Texas has become the most expensive on record, with John Cornyn and James Talarico heavily outspending their rivals. But Mr. Cornyn seems to be in trouble.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer

item.title: U.S. Sends More Troops to the Mideast as Iran War Expands
item.description: Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, acknowledged the possibility of an extended campaign, as the military announced that six U.S. service members had been killed so far.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Associated Press
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, during the Trump administration’s first news conference on the war in Iran, on Monday.

item.title: Texas Primary Early Voting Figures Show High Democratic Turnout
item.description: More than 1.3 million voters cast ballots in the Democratic primary during the early voting period that ended Friday. Many counties hadn’t yet reported their data.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk

item.title: Austin Shooting Puts Islam at Center of Tuesday’s Primaries in Texas
item.description: Politicians quickly cited the attack in their campaign messaging. Some Republicans called for stopping immigration, while Democrats called for gun control.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Tova Katzman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The attack unfolded early Sunday morning at a bar near the University of Texas.

item.title: Texas Tech Student Identified as One of the Victims in Austin Shooting
item.description: Ryder Harrington, 19, Savitha Shan, 21, and Jorge Pederson, 30, were confirmed dead by officials on Monday. The shooting is being investigated as a possible act of terrorism.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao, Lina Fisher and Ramón Ramirez
item.media:credit: Tova Katzman for The New York Times
item.media:description: A shooting occurred at Buford’s, a popular bar near the University of Texas at Austin campus, early on Sunday.

item.title: The attack on Iran prompted swift responses from candidates with primaries looming.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump on Sunday. Midterm candidates released a flurry of videos, social media posts and statements responding to news of the strikes.

item.title: Latino Democrats Fight to Fend Off Upstart Challengers
item.description: The Congressional Hispanic Caucus is defending four key incumbents in primary races centered on how aggressively Democrats are combating ICE and President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Adriano Espaillat of New York arriving to conduct an oversight visit of an immigration detention facility in Manhattan in December.

item.title: The Unexpected Winners From Trump’s New Global Tariff
item.description: The president’s flat 10 percent tariff is most beneficial to nations that previously faced the highest rates. But it’s not clear how much that will prompt a new surge in imports.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Lazaro Gamio
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina and Nick Corasaniti

item.title: The Battle for Congress Is Kicking Off. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: Primary contests in Texas could help decide control of the House and Senate in November, and North Carolina and Arkansas are also holding elections on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: Texas Primary Elections: What to Know, How to Vote, ID Rules and More
item.description: The primaries in the race for a U.S. Senate seat will be closely watched on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Robinson
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Texans are heading to the polls to choose candidates for federal and state offices.

item.title: 6 Conservative Voters React to Attacks on Iran Ahead of the Texas Primaries
item.description: President Trump said the attacks were necessary for U.S. security and to free the Iranian people from oppression. Do people who support him agree?
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mariano Castillo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nate McHale

item.title: Honored Nationwide, Jesse Jackson Is Less Celebrated in His Hometown
item.description: As Mr. Jackson’s body lies in state in South Carolina on Monday, the city of Greenville, where he fought civil rights battles, is wrestling with how to pay its respects.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: The home where Jesse Jackson was born in Greenville, S.C.

item.title: 5 Takeaways on America’s Boom in Billionaires
item.description: The number of billionaires in the United States has soared, with nowhere feeling the effects quite like Jackson, Wyo. Here’s where all that money came from.
item.dc:creator: Katie Benner and Steven Rich
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction of a home in Jackson, Wyo.

item.title: Five Testy Moments From the Acrimonious Texas Senate Primaries
item.description: Ahead of Tuesday’s vote, the Democratic and Republican races have become increasingly personal and contentious.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Jasmine Crockett is locked in a competitive Senate primary.

item.title: Republicans Cite Iran Attacks to Pressure Democrats on Shutdown
item.description: GOP lawmakers ensured there was enough money for the immigration crackdown, but the funding pause threatens other Homeland Security work.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents raiding a home in Minneapolis last month.

item.title: Supreme Court Appears Skeptical of Law Banning Drug Users From Owning Guns
item.description: A majority of the justices voiced concerns that the law may be overbroad, lumping together occasional drug users with addicts who threaten public safety.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Shelby Tauber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The case before the justices involved Ali Hemani, a Texas gun owner arrested after he admitted to using marijuana.

item.title: Could a Supreme Court Case Help Hunter Biden Get His Law License Back?
item.description: The former president’s son says he is watching closely as the justices on Monday hear a case testing the constitutionality of the federal gun law used to convict him.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hunter Biden outside the courthouse during his gun charges trial in Wilmington, Del., in 2024.

item.title: Labor Secretary’s Tenure Marked by Frequent Travel and Internal Tumult
item.description: Lori Chavez-DeRemer is under investigation internally after allegations of misconduct and misuse of department funds.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien and Evan Gorelick
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lori Chavez-DeRemer, President Trump’s labor secretary, has led the Labor Department into crisis.

item.title: An N.F.L. Kicker, a Yankee and a Sports Reporter Walk Into the Midterms
item.description: As podcasts and talk radio blur the lines between sports and politics, professional athletes — many of whom have found encouragement from President Trump — are running in midterm elections.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Gay/Associated Press; Mary Kouw/CBS, via Getty Images; Tyler Kaufman/Associated Press
item.media:description: Mark Teixeira, the former New York Yankee; Jay Feely, the former N.F.L. kicker; and Michele Tafoya, formerly a reporter for NBC, ABC and ESPN; are just a few candidates for political offices this year who come from sports backgrounds.
